title: Backpropagation
description: The backward pass that applies the chain rule on a computational graph to compute gradients for every differentiable parameter.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: Backpropagation (often shortened to backprop) is reverse-mode automatic differentiation specialized for neural networks. Starting at the loss node, the framework multiplies local Jacobians along edges of the computational graph so earlier parameters receive credit for how they influenced the final error.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: It makes deep networks trainable without hand-derived derivatives for every layer. The same code that computed logits, softmax, and cross-entropy loss can call backward once and populate gradients on embeddings, attention projections, and output weights consistently.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: Consider logits → softmax → cross-entropy. Backpropagation first yields ∂L/∂logits, then continues through earlier matmuls and activations. Each step applies the chain rule so ∂L/∂W for a weight matrix W combines upstream loss sensitivity with the forward input that multiplied W.

whereItAppears.title: Where It Appears
whereItAppears.body: Every mainstream training stack exposes backprop via `.backward()`, `grad`, or `value_and_grad`. Checkpointing trades extra forward recomputation for memory by not storing every activation. Distinctive chain phrase: backward pass through the autograd graph after forward logits.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: Backpropagation is not the optimizer step—it only computes gradients. It is also not forward inference: no weight updates happen during backward unless you explicitly call an optimizer. Reverse-mode autodiff is efficient for many parameters and one scalar loss, which is the usual language-model training setup.

openingSummary: Backpropagation reuses the forward graph in reverse so each op propagates loss derivatives into its inputs until every parameter receives a gradient for the optimizer.
